meta.notes: Novel AI-generated PSBB practice questions for the SpeedyCAT proof-of-concept. NOT real MCAT/AAMC content. Text-only. Chunk 6 of 10.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of cultural relativism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The smartphones, cars, and clothing produced and used within a society.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Withholding judgment of an unfamiliar ritual until one understands its meaning within that culture.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A community losing its shared rules and sense of order after sudden upheaval.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Assuming that eating with one's hands instead of utensils is simply backward and wrong.

questions.explanation: An example of cultural relativism is: withholding judgment of an unfamiliar ritual until one understands its meaning within that culture. In general, cultural relativism refers to the practice of understanding a culture by its own standards rather than judging it by another culture's. The other options illustrate different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which term is best described as follows: the delay between a change in material culture and the adjustment of nonmaterial culture to it?

choices.label: A
choices.text: beliefs

choices.label: B
choices.text: high culture

choices.label: C
choices.text: multiculturalism

choices.label: D
choices.text: cultural lag

questions.explanation: This description matches cultural lag, which is the delay between a change in material culture and the adjustment of nonmaterial culture to it. The other options name different concepts that do not fit this description.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes multiculturalism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A deeply negative label that reshapes a person's self-concept and social identity.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The mechanisms a society uses to encourage or compel conformity to its norms.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The spread of cultural traits, ideas, or objects from one group or society to another.

choices.label: D
choices.text: An approach that encourages distinct cultural groups to keep their identities within one society.

questions.explanation: Multiculturalism is best described as an approach that encourages distinct cultural groups to keep their identities within one society. The other options describe different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of a taboo?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The near-universal horror people feel toward cannibalism.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sweeping public alarm that a new video game is corrupting the nation's youth.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Wearing very casual clothing to a formal dinner and drawing disapproving looks.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A concert crowd suddenly surging together toward the stage.

questions.explanation: An example of a taboo is: the near-universal horror people feel toward cannibalism. In general, a taboo refers to a norm so deeply held that its violation provokes extreme disgust or revulsion. The other options illustrate different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes strain theory?

choices.label: C

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of relative deprivation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Feeling poor despite a solid income because one's neighbors are far wealthier.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The same coffee chain and phone brand appearing in cities on every continent.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Wearing very casual clothing to a formal dinner and drawing disapproving looks.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hit songs, blockbuster films, and viral videos enjoyed by the general public.

questions.explanation: An example of relative deprivation is: feeling poor despite a solid income because one's neighbors are far wealthier. In general, relative deprivation refers to the perception of being worse off compared with the others one uses as a reference. The other options illustrate different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of popular culture?

choices.text: Hit songs, blockbuster films, and viral videos enjoyed by the general public.

choices.text: A dish from one country becoming a popular menu item in distant nations.

questions.explanation: An example of popular culture is: hit songs, blockbuster films, and viral videos enjoyed by the general public. questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of glutamate?

questions.stem: Seven employees earn these annual salaries in thousands of dollars: 40, 42, 44, 45, 47, 49, and 220. Which statement best describes these data?

choices.label: B
choices.text: The mean and median are equal for these data.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The mean is the best summary here because it is unaffected by extreme values.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The median of 45 represents the typical salary better than the mean, because one very high value pulls the mean upward.

questions.explanation: The mean is about 69.6 (487 divided by 7), while the median is 45. The single outlier of 220 inflates the mean, so the median better reflects the typical salary.

questions.stem: Across neighborhoods, the number of ice cream cones sold is positively correlated with the number of swimming-pool accidents. Which explanation is most appropriate?

choices.text: Eating ice cream causes swimming-pool accidents.

choices.text: Swimming-pool accidents cause people to buy ice cream.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The correlation proves that banning ice cream would reduce accidents.

questions.explanation: A positive correlation can arise from a confounding third variable, here hot weather, that drives both measures; correlation does not imply causation.
